title: How to Calculate BMI: Formula, Chart & Interpretation Guide
description: Learn how to calculate Body Mass Index (BMI) step by step. Understand the BMI formula, categories, and what your results mean for your health.
keywords: how to calculate BMI, BMI formula, body mass index calculator, BMI chart
h1: How to Calculate BMI: A Complete Step-by-Step Guide
content:
Body Mass Index (BMI) is one of the most widely used screening tools to assess whether a person has a healthy body weight relative to their height. Whether you're tracking your fitness goals or simply curious about where you stand, understanding how to calculate BMI is a valuable skill.

What Is BMI?

BMI is a numerical value derived from your weight and height. It was developed by Belgian mathematician Adolphe Quetelet in the 1830s and remains a standard metric used by healthcare professionals worldwide to categorize individuals into weight status groups.

The BMI Formula

There are two versions of the BMI formula depending on your measurement system:

Metric Formula

BMI = weight (kg) ÷ height² (m²)

For example, if you weigh 70 kg and are 1.75 m tall:
BMI = 70 ÷ (1.75 × 1.75) = 70 ÷ 3.0625 = 22.9

Imperial Formula

BMI = (weight (lbs) × 703) ÷ height² (inches²)

For example, if you weigh 154 lbs and are 69 inches tall:
BMI = (154 × 703) ÷ (69 × 69) = 108,262 ÷ 4,761 = 22.7

BMI Categories

Once you've calculated your BMI, here's how to interpret the result:

  • Below 18.5 — Underweight
  • 18.5 to 24.9 — Normal weight
  • 25.0 to 29.9 — Overweight
  • 30.0 and above — Obese

Step-by-Step Calculation Example

Let's walk through a complete example. Sarah weighs 65 kg and is 1.68 m tall.

  1. Square the height: 1.68 × 1.68 = 2.8224
  2. Divide weight by height squared: 65 ÷ 2.8224 = 23.03
  3. Check the category: 23.03 falls in the "Normal weight" range

Limitations of BMI

While BMI is useful as a quick screening tool, it has limitations. It doesn't distinguish between muscle mass and fat mass, doesn't account for bone density, and may not be accurate for athletes, elderly individuals, or pregnant women. Always consult a healthcare professional for a comprehensive health assessment.

Tips for Maintaining a Healthy BMI

  • Eat a balanced diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and lean proteins
  • Exercise regularly — aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate activity per week
  • Monitor your weight periodically
  • Get adequate sleep (7-9 hours per night)

title: How to Calculate Loan Payments: Monthly Payment Formula Explained
description: Learn how to calculate monthly loan payments using the amortization formula. Understand interest rates, loan terms, and total cost of borrowing.
keywords: how to calculate loan payment, monthly payment formula, loan amortization, mortgage calculator
h1: How to Calculate Loan Payments: Monthly Payment Formula Explained
content:
Whether you're buying a home, financing a car, or taking out a personal loan, understanding how your monthly payment is calculated helps you make smarter financial decisions. This guide breaks down the loan payment formula step by step.

The Monthly Payment Formula

The standard formula for calculating a fixed monthly loan payment is:

M = P × [r(1+r)ⁿ] ÷ [(1+r)ⁿ - 1]

Where:

  • M = Monthly payment
  • P = Principal (loan amount)
  • r = Monthly interest rate (annual rate ÷ 12)
  • n = Total number of payments (loan term in months)

Step-by-Step Example

Let's calculate the monthly payment for a $200,000 mortgage at 6.5% annual interest over 30 years.

  1. Convert annual rate to monthly: 6.5% ÷ 12 = 0.5417% = 0.005417
  2. Calculate total payments: 30 years × 12 = 360 months
  3. Apply the formula:
    M = 200,000 × [0.005417(1.005417)³⁶⁰] ÷ [(1.005417)³⁶⁰ - 1]
    M = 200,000 × [0.005417 × 6.9918] ÷ [6.9918 - 1]
    M = 200,000 × 0.03788 ÷ 5.9918
    M = $1,264

Understanding Amortization

In the early years of a loan, most of your payment goes toward interest. Over time, more goes toward principal. This is called amortization. For our $200,000 example:

  • First payment: $1,083 interest + $181 principal
  • Final payment: $7 interest + $1,257 principal
  • Total paid over 30 years: $455,089 (that's $255,089 in interest!)

How Interest Rate Affects Your Payment

Even small rate differences have a big impact over time:

  • $200,000 at 5.5% for 30 years = $1,136/month (total: $408,808)
  • $200,000 at 6.5% for 30 years = $1,264/month (total: $455,089)
  • $200,000 at 7.5% for 30 years = $1,398/month (total: $503,434)

A 2% rate difference costs you nearly $95,000 more over the life of the loan.

Tips to Reduce Your Loan Cost

  • Make extra payments toward principal when possible
  • Choose a shorter loan term if you can afford higher payments
  • Shop around for the best interest rate
  • Consider refinancing if rates drop significantly
  • Avoid loans with prepayment penalties

title: How to Calculate Percentages: 5 Easy Methods with Examples
description: Master percentage calculations with 5 simple methods. Learn to find percentages, calculate discounts, percentage change, and more with real-world examples.
keywords: how to calculate percentage, percentage formula, find percentage of number, percentage change
h1: How to Calculate Percentages: 5 Easy Methods with Examples
content:
Percentages are everywhere — from shopping discounts to exam scores to financial reports. Mastering percentage calculations is an essential life skill. Here are five common percentage problems and how to solve them.

Method 1: Finding a Percentage of a Number

Formula: Result = (Percentage ÷ 100) × Number

Example: What is 15% of 200?
Result = (15 ÷ 100) × 200 = 0.15 × 200 = 30

Method 2: Finding What Percentage One Number Is of Another

Formula: Percentage = (Part ÷ Whole) × 100

Example: 45 is what percent of 180?
Percentage = (45 ÷ 180) × 100 = 0.25 × 100 = 25%

Method 3: Percentage Increase

Formula: Increase % = [(New - Original) ÷ Original] × 100

Example: A stock went from $50 to $65. What's the percentage increase?
Increase = [(65 - 50) ÷ 50] × 100 = (15 ÷ 50) × 100 = 30%

Method 4: Percentage Decrease

Formula: Decrease % = [(Original - New) ÷ Original] × 100

Example: A product dropped from $80 to $60. What's the percentage decrease?
Decrease = [(80 - 60) ÷ 80] × 100 = (20 ÷ 80) × 100 = 25%

Method 5: Reverse Percentage (Finding the Original)

Formula: Original = Final ÷ (1 + Percentage/100)

Example: After a 20% markup, an item costs $60. What was the original price?
Original = 60 ÷ (1 + 20/100) = 60 ÷ 1.2 = $50

Quick Mental Math Tricks

  • 10%: Move the decimal one place left (10% of 450 = 45)
  • 5%: Find 10% and halve it (5% of 450 = 22.5)
  • 25%: Divide by 4 (25% of 200 = 50)
  • 1%: Move decimal two places left (1% of 450 = 4.5)
  • Any %: Combine the above (15% = 10% + 5%)

Common Real-World Applications

  • Shopping: Calculate sale prices and discounts
  • Tipping: Figure out restaurant tips quickly
  • Finance: Understand interest rates and returns
  • Grades: Convert scores to percentages
  • Cooking: Scale recipes up or down

title: How to Calculate Age: From Birthdate to Exact Years, Months & Days
description: Learn how to calculate exact age from a birthdate including years, months, and days. Understand age calculation methods for different purposes.
keywords: how to calculate age, age from birthdate, exact age calculator, age in days
h1: How to Calculate Age: Exact Years, Months & Days from Birthdate
content:
Calculating age seems simple — but getting the exact age in years, months, and days requires careful handling of varying month lengths and leap years. This guide shows you how to do it accurately.

Basic Age Calculation

The simplest method: subtract the birth year from the current year, then adjust if the birthday hasn't occurred yet this year.

Example: Born March 15, 1990. Today is May 28, 2026.
2026 - 1990 = 36 years. Since May 28 is after March 15, the age is 36 years.

Calculating Exact Age (Years, Months, Days)

For a precise calculation:

  1. Years: Subtract birth year from current year. If current date is before birthday this year, subtract 1.
  2. Months: Count months from last birthday. If current day is less than birth day, subtract 1 month and add days from previous month.
  3. Days: Count remaining days.

Detailed Example

Born: January 20, 1995. Today: May 28, 2026.

  1. Years: 2026 - 1995 = 31 (birthday already passed in January)
  2. Months: January → May = 4 months. Since 28 ≥ 20, we keep 4 months.
  3. Days: 28 - 20 = 8 days

Result: 31 years, 4 months, 8 days

Handling Edge Cases

Leap Year Birthdays

If born on February 29, most systems count the birthday as March 1 in non-leap years. Some legal jurisdictions use February 28 instead.

Month-End Dates

Born on January 31 — what happens in February? Since February has only 28/29 days, the "monthly anniversary" is typically the last day of February.

Age in Different Units

  • Age in months: Years × 12 + remaining months
  • Age in weeks: Total days ÷ 7
  • Age in days: Count each day from birth to today (accounting for leap years)
  • Age in hours: Days × 24

Why Accurate Age Matters

  • Legal purposes: Voting age, drinking age, retirement eligibility
  • Medical: Pediatric growth charts, medication dosing
  • Insurance: Premium calculations based on exact age
  • Education: School enrollment cutoff dates

title: How to Calculate Tip: Quick Methods for Any Restaurant Bill
description: Learn easy methods to calculate tips at restaurants. From the 10% base method to splitting bills, master tipping math in seconds.
keywords: how to calculate tip, tip calculator, restaurant tip percentage, tipping guide
h1: How to Calculate Tip: Quick Methods for Any Restaurant Bill
content:
Calculating a tip shouldn't require pulling out your phone's calculator app. With a few simple mental math tricks, you can figure out the right tip amount in seconds. Here's how.

The Standard Tip Percentages

  • 15% — Acceptable service
  • 18% — Good service
  • 20% — Great service
  • 25%+ — Exceptional service

The 10% Base Method

The easiest mental math approach uses 10% as your building block:

  1. Find 10% by moving the decimal point one place left
  2. Adjust from there

Example: $67.50 bill

  • 10% = $6.75
  • 15% = $6.75 + $3.38 (half of 10%) = $10.13
  • 20% = $6.75 × 2 = $13.50
  • 25% = $6.75 × 2 + $3.38 = $16.88

The Double-Tax Method

In many US states, sales tax is around 8-10%. Simply double the tax amount for an approximate 16-20% tip. Quick and easy!

The Round-Up Method

For simplicity, round your bill to the nearest $10, then calculate:

Bill: $47.30 → Round to $50
20% of $50 = $10 (slightly generous, which is fine)

Splitting the Bill with Tip

Formula: (Bill + Tip) ÷ Number of People

Example: $120 bill, 20% tip, 4 people:
Tip = $24. Total = $144. Per person = $144 ÷ 4 = $36 each

Pre-Tax vs. Post-Tax Tipping

Technically, you should tip on the pre-tax amount. But the difference is usually small (a few cents to a dollar), so most people tip on the total for simplicity.

When to Tip More or Less

  • Tip more: Large groups, complex orders, holidays, bad weather delivery
  • Tip standard: Normal dine-in, takeout with counter service
  • No tip expected: Fast food, self-service, some countries outside the US

International Tipping Guide

  • USA/Canada: 15-25% expected
  • UK: 10-15% (often included as service charge)
  • Japan: No tipping (can be considered rude)
  • Europe: Round up or 5-10%
  • Australia: Not expected, 10% for great service

title: How to Convert Temperature: Celsius, Fahrenheit & Kelvin Formulas
description: Learn all temperature conversion formulas between Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin with easy examples and memory tricks.
keywords: how to convert temperature, Celsius to Fahrenheit, Fahrenheit to Celsius, temperature conversion formula
h1: How to Convert Temperature: Celsius, Fahrenheit & Kelvin Formulas
content:
Temperature conversion is one of the most common unit conversions people need. Whether you're traveling abroad, cooking with an international recipe, or studying science, knowing how to convert between Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin is essential.

The Three Temperature Scales

  • Celsius (°C): Used worldwide for everyday temperatures. Water freezes at 0°C, boils at 100°C.
  • Fahrenheit (°F): Used in the US for weather and cooking. Water freezes at 32°F, boils at 212°F.
  • Kelvin (K): Used in science. Absolute zero (no thermal energy) = 0 K = -273.15°C.

Conversion Formulas

Celsius to Fahrenheit

°F = (°C × 9/5) + 32

Example: Convert 25°C to Fahrenheit
°F = (25 × 9/5) + 32 = 45 + 32 = 77°F

Fahrenheit to Celsius

°C = (°F - 32) × 5/9

Example: Convert 98.6°F to Celsius
°C = (98.6 - 32) × 5/9 = 66.6 × 5/9 = 37°C

Celsius to Kelvin

K = °C + 273.15

Example: Convert 100°C to Kelvin
K = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 K

Kelvin to Celsius

°C = K - 273.15

Fahrenheit to Kelvin

K = (°F - 32) × 5/9 + 273.15

Quick Reference Points

  • -40° is the same in both Celsius and Fahrenheit
  • 0°C = 32°F (water freezes)
  • 37°C = 98.6°F (body temperature)
  • 100°C = 212°F (water boils)

Mental Math Shortcuts

For a rough Celsius to Fahrenheit conversion: Double it and add 30.

  • 20°C → 20×2+30 = 70°F (actual: 68°F — close enough!)
  • 30°C → 30×2+30 = 90°F (actual: 86°F)

For Fahrenheit to Celsius: Subtract 30, then halve it.

  • 80°F → (80-30)÷2 = 25°C (actual: 26.7°C)

Common Cooking Temperatures

  • 180°C = 356°F (baking cakes)
  • 200°C = 392°F (roasting vegetables)
  • 220°C = 428°F (pizza)
  • Gas Mark 4 = 180°C = 350°F

title: How to Calculate Discount: Sale Price Formula & Stacking Discounts
description: Learn how to calculate discounts, sale prices, and stacking multiple discounts. Master the math behind every sale with simple formulas.
keywords: how to calculate discount, sale price formula, percentage off calculator, stacking discounts
h1: How to Calculate Discount: Sale Price Formula & Stacking Discounts
content:
Sales and discounts are everywhere, but do you really know how much you're saving? Understanding discount math helps you spot genuine deals and avoid marketing tricks.

Basic Discount Formula

Sale Price = Original Price × (1 - Discount%/100)

Or equivalently:
Discount Amount = Original Price × (Discount%/100)
Sale Price = Original Price - Discount Amount

Example

A $80 jacket is 30% off:
Discount = $80 × 0.30 = $24
Sale Price = $80 - $24 = $56

Finding the Discount Percentage

Discount % = [(Original - Sale Price) ÷ Original] × 100

Example: Was $120, now $84:
Discount % = [(120 - 84) ÷ 120] × 100 = (36 ÷ 120) × 100 = 30%

Stacking Multiple Discounts

When stores offer "extra 20% off sale items," the discounts multiply — they don't add.

Formula: Final Price = Original × (1 - D1/100) × (1 - D2/100)

Example

$100 item, 40% off + extra 20% off:
After first discount: $100 × 0.60 = $60
After second discount: $60 × 0.80 = $48

⚠️ This is NOT the same as 60% off ($40). Stacked 40%+20% = 52% total discount, not 60%.

Buy-One-Get-One (BOGO) Deals

  • BOGO Free: Effectively 50% off when buying 2
  • BOGO 50% off: Effectively 25% off when buying 2
  • Buy 2 Get 1 Free: Effectively 33% off when buying 3

Is It Really a Good Deal?

Ask yourself these questions:

  • Would you buy it at full price? If not, any discount on something you don't need isn't saving money.
  • What's the price per unit? Bulk deals aren't always cheaper.
  • Is the "original price" inflated? Some retailers raise prices before sales.
  • Can you find it cheaper elsewhere even without the discount?

title: How to Convert Length Units: Metric & Imperial Conversion Guide
description: Complete guide to converting between meters, feet, inches, kilometers, and miles. Includes formulas, conversion factors, and quick reference tables.
keywords: how to convert length units, meters to feet, km to miles, inches to cm, unit conversion
h1: How to Convert Length Units: Metric & Imperial Conversion Guide
content:
Length conversion between metric and imperial systems is one of the most common everyday calculations. Whether you're shopping for furniture, following a workout plan, or reading international news, this guide covers all the conversions you need.

Key Conversion Factors

  • 1 inch = 2.54 cm
  • 1 foot = 30.48 cm = 0.3048 m
  • 1 yard = 0.9144 m
  • 1 mile = 1.60934 km
  • 1 meter = 3.28084 feet
  • 1 km = 0.621371 miles

Metric to Imperial Conversions

Centimeters to Inches

inches = cm ÷ 2.54

Example: 170 cm to inches = 170 ÷ 2.54 = 66.93 inches (5 feet 6.93 inches)

Meters to Feet

feet = meters × 3.28084

Example: 1.8 m = 1.8 × 3.28084 = 5.91 feet (5 feet 10.9 inches)

Kilometers to Miles

miles = km × 0.621371

Example: 10 km = 10 × 0.621371 = 6.21 miles

Imperial to Metric Conversions

Inches to Centimeters

cm = inches × 2.54

Example: 72 inches = 72 × 2.54 = 182.88 cm

Feet to Meters

meters = feet × 0.3048

Example: 6 feet = 6 × 0.3048 = 1.8288 m

Miles to Kilometers

km = miles × 1.60934

Example: 26.2 miles (marathon) = 26.2 × 1.60934 = 42.16 km

Quick Mental Math Tricks

  • km to miles: Multiply by 0.6 (or use Fibonacci — consecutive numbers approximate the conversion: 5 km ≈ 3 miles, 8 km ≈ 5 miles)
  • cm to inches: Divide by 2.5 for a quick estimate
  • meters to feet: Multiply by 3 and add 10%

Height Conversion Table

  • 5'0" = 152 cm
  • 5'4" = 163 cm
  • 5'8" = 173 cm
  • 6'0" = 183 cm
  • 6'4" = 193 cm

When You Need Length Conversions

  • Travel: Road signs, distances, speed limits
  • Fitness: Running distances, pool lengths
  • Home improvement: Furniture dimensions, room measurements
  • Clothing: International size charts

title: How to Convert Weight Units: Pounds, Kilograms, Ounces & Grams
description: Learn to convert between pounds, kilograms, ounces, and grams with simple formulas. Includes cooking measurements and international shipping weights.
keywords: how to convert weight, pounds to kg, kg to lbs, ounces to grams, weight conversion
h1: How to Convert Weight Units: Pounds, Kilograms, Ounces & Grams
content:
Weight conversion between metric and imperial systems comes up constantly — from tracking fitness goals to cooking international recipes to shipping packages. Here's your complete guide to weight unit conversions.

Key Conversion Factors

  • 1 pound (lb) = 0.453592 kg
  • 1 kilogram (kg) = 2.20462 lbs
  • 1 ounce (oz) = 28.3495 grams
  • 1 gram = 0.035274 oz
  • 1 stone = 14 pounds = 6.35 kg
  • 1 ton (US) = 2,000 lbs = 907.185 kg

Pounds to Kilograms

kg = lbs × 0.453592

Example: 150 lbs = 150 × 0.4536 = 68.04 kg

Quick trick: Divide by 2, then subtract 10% of that result.
150 ÷ 2 = 75, minus 10% (7.5) = 67.5 kg (close to actual 68.04)

Kilograms to Pounds

lbs = kg × 2.20462

Example: 75 kg = 75 × 2.2046 = 165.35 lbs

Quick trick: Double it, then add 10% of the original.
75 × 2 = 150, plus 10% of 75 (7.5) = 157.5 lbs (rough estimate)

Ounces and Grams

Ounces to Grams

grams = oz × 28.3495

Example: 8 oz = 8 × 28.35 = 226.8 grams

Grams to Ounces

oz = grams ÷ 28.3495

Example: 500 g = 500 ÷ 28.35 = 17.64 oz

Cooking Weight Conversions

  • 1 cup flour ≈ 125 g ≈ 4.4 oz
  • 1 cup sugar ≈ 200 g ≈ 7 oz
  • 1 cup butter ≈ 227 g ≈ 8 oz (2 sticks)
  • 1 tablespoon ≈ 15 g (for most ingredients)

Body Weight Context

  • Average adult male: 70-90 kg (154-198 lbs)
  • Average adult female: 55-75 kg (121-165 lbs)
  • Newborn baby: 3-4 kg (6.6-8.8 lbs)

Shipping & Luggage

  • Carry-on luggage limit: typically 7-10 kg (15-22 lbs)
  • Checked bag limit: typically 23 kg (50 lbs)
  • Overweight surcharge: usually above 32 kg (70 lbs)

title: How to Calculate Fuel Cost for a Trip: MPG, Distance & Gas Price Formula
description: Calculate your trip fuel cost using distance, fuel efficiency, and gas price. Learn MPG vs L/100km and tips to reduce fuel expenses.
keywords: how to calculate fuel cost, trip gas cost, fuel efficiency calculator, MPG calculator
h1: How to Calculate Fuel Cost for a Trip: Complete Guide
content:
Planning a road trip? Knowing your fuel cost in advance helps you budget properly. Here's how to calculate exactly how much gas money you'll need.

The Basic Formula (US/Imperial)

Fuel Cost = (Distance ÷ MPG) × Price per Gallon

Example

Driving 300 miles, car gets 28 MPG, gas costs $3.50/gallon:
Fuel needed = 300 ÷ 28 = 10.71 gallons
Cost = 10.71 × $3.50 = $37.50

The Metric Formula

Fuel Cost = (Distance × L/100km ÷ 100) × Price per Liter

Example

Driving 500 km, car uses 7.5 L/100km, fuel costs $1.80/liter:
Fuel needed = 500 × 7.5 ÷ 100 = 37.5 liters
Cost = 37.5 × $1.80 = $67.50

Converting Between MPG and L/100km

  • MPG to L/100km: 235.215 ÷ MPG = L/100km
  • L/100km to MPG: 235.215 ÷ L/100km = MPG

Example: 30 MPG = 235.215 ÷ 30 = 7.84 L/100km

Round Trip Calculation

Don't forget to double the distance for round trips!

Example: 150 miles each way at 25 MPG, $3.80/gallon:
Total distance = 300 miles
Cost = (300 ÷ 25) × $3.80 = 12 × $3.80 = $45.60

Comparing Fuel Costs: Gas vs. Hybrid vs. Electric

  • Gas (28 MPG): 300 miles = 10.7 gal × $3.50 = $37.50
  • Hybrid (50 MPG): 300 miles = 6 gal × $3.50 = $21.00
  • Electric (3.5 mi/kWh): 300 miles = 85.7 kWh × $0.13 = $11.14

Tips to Reduce Fuel Costs

  • Maintain steady speed — use cruise control on highways
  • Keep tires properly inflated (saves 3% fuel)
  • Remove excess weight from your vehicle
  • Avoid aggressive acceleration and braking
  • Plan routes to avoid traffic and hills
  • Use apps like GasBuddy to find cheapest stations

Splitting Gas on Group Trips

Per person cost = Total fuel cost ÷ Number of passengers

A 500-mile trip at $75 fuel cost with 4 people = $18.75 each. Much cheaper than flying!

title: 10 Best Free Online Calculators You Need in 2026
description: Discover the top 10 free online calculators for everyday math, finance, health, and unit conversions. No downloads, no sign-ups required.
keywords: best online calculators, free calculator tools, online math tools 2026, calculator website
h1: 10 Best Free Online Calculators You Need in 2026
content:
Gone are the days of hunting for a physical calculator or struggling with complex spreadsheet formulas. Today's online calculators handle everything from splitting a dinner bill to planning your mortgage. Here are the 10 most useful free online calculators you should bookmark.

1. BMI Calculator

Track your health with a Body Mass Index calculator. Simply enter your height and weight to get your BMI score and understand which category you fall into. Essential for anyone monitoring their fitness journey.

2. Loan Payment Calculator

Before signing any loan agreement, use a loan calculator to understand your monthly payments, total interest, and the true cost of borrowing. Works for mortgages, car loans, and personal loans.

3. Percentage Calculator

The percentage calculator handles all percentage math: finding percentages, calculating increases/decreases, and reverse percentage problems. Perfect for shopping, grades, and financial analysis.

4. Age Calculator

Need your exact age in years, months, and days? The age calculator handles leap years and varying month lengths automatically. Useful for legal documents, insurance, and milestone tracking.

5. Tip Calculator

Never awkwardly fumble with tip math again. The tip calculator handles any percentage and splits bills evenly among groups. Essential for dining out.

6. Temperature Converter

Traveling between countries that use different temperature scales? The temperature converter instantly converts between Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin.

7. Discount Calculator

Is that "70% off" deal really worth it? The discount calculator shows you the actual sale price and savings amount. It even handles stacked discounts.

8. Length Unit Converter

Convert between meters, feet, inches, kilometers, and miles with the length converter. Indispensable for international shopping, travel, and home improvement projects.

9. Weight Unit Converter

Switch between pounds, kilograms, ounces, and grams using the weight converter. Great for cooking, fitness tracking, and shipping calculations.

10. Fuel Cost Calculator

Planning a road trip? The fuel cost calculator estimates your gas expenses based on distance, fuel efficiency, and current gas prices. Budget your trip accurately.

Why Use Online Calculators?

  • Always available: Access from any device with a browser
  • No downloads: No apps to install or update
  • Free: No subscriptions or hidden fees
  • Accurate: Tested formulas eliminate human error
  • Fast: Get results instantly without manual math

Tips for Getting the Most from Online Calculators

  • Bookmark your most-used calculators for quick access
  • Double-check inputs — garbage in, garbage out
  • Understand the formula behind the calculator (our blog explains each one)
  • Use them for verification even when you can do mental math
